content.text:
## Phase 1 — Analysis (Fable 5)

All four analyses are grounded directly in the current code (each agent independently verified — and in two cases corrected — my earlier scouting pass: engine.go not segments.go for the cycle global, and the merge.go `nextExtends` preservation detail).

### #7594 — [project segment multiple project files](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7594)
`Enabled()` in [project.go](src/segments/project.go) builds a hardcoded 13-item priority order and returns on first match — `package.json` always beats `composer.json` when both exist. No priority/aggregation knob exists today.
**Fix:** new `priority` string-array option that reorders the existing type list; unlisted types stay appended after (backward compatible).

### #7531 — [multi-level extends](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7531)
N-level chaining **already works** — `Parse` in [load.go](src/config/load.go) loops correctly and `merge.go` already preserves the next hop's `Extends`. Two real gaps: no cycle detection (A→B→A hangs forever), and relative paths in hop 2+ resolve against the *first* config's directory, not their own — breaks the reporter's exact proxy-file layout if the proxy file isn't co-located with the main config.
**Fix:** add a visited-path guard, and re-anchor `parentFolder` after each hop.

### #6334 — [path segment multiple capture groups](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6334)
`handleRegex` in [path.go](src/segments/path.go) hardcodes capture-group index 1 and does a plain `strings.Replace`. The Fable agent also flagged a **latent bug independent of this feature**: `strings.Replace` rewrites the *first textual occurrence* of the captured substring, not the occurrence at the actual match position — so a path like `/home/src/project/src/deep` with a `src`-capturing regex can mutate the wrong `src`.
**Fix:** opt-in `mapped_locations_regex_expand` boolean (the reporter's own option 2), using Go's existing `regex.ReplaceAllString` with `$1`/`${name}` expansion. Legacy behavior (including the latent bug) stays frozen by default — the bug is real but fixing default behavior is a separate, riskier decision I'm leaving alone here.

### #5442 — [color cycle exclusion/reset](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/5442)
The color cycle is one shared, mutable pointer consumed in render order across every block — a second-line block starts wherever the first line's segment count left off. Confirmed at [segments.go:191](src/prompt/segments.go) and [engine.go:316](src/prompt/engine.go).
**Fix:** new `restart_cycle` boolean on `Block` that re-points the cycle at the pristine config value before that block renders.

**Design calls I'm making** (each was an open question the analyses raised, and each is a judgment call rather than something requiring your product sign-off, so I'm pinning them rather than looping back):
- Unrecognized `priority` names: ignored silently (no hard failure on a typo).
- Extends cycle: `log.Error` + break, matching the existing failure convention in `load.go`, not a new error type.
- `mapped_locations_regex_expand`: exactly the name and shape the reporter proposed — it already matches the `mapped_locations_*` naming convention in the codebase.
- All four are backward compatible by default — no existing config's behavior changes.

Moving into Plan → Delegate now, one Sonnet 5 implementer per issue in its own isolated worktree, running in parallel since the four touch non-overlapping code paths.

input.description: Implement issue 7594 project priority
input.prompt:
You are the implementer for one pinned task in the oh-my-posh Go project (module root is `src/`, not repo root — run all go commands from `src/`). You're in an isolated git worktree. Follow the repo's `.claude/skills/golang/SKILL.md` conventions exactly (early returns, no `else`, table-driven tests, etc).

## Task: GitHub issue #7594 — project segment, multiple project files

**Problem:** `Enabled()` in `src/segments/project.go` builds a hardcoded ordered slice of `ProjectItem`s (node, deno, jsr, cargo, python, mojo, php/composer, dart, nuspec, dotnet, julia, lake, powershell) and returns on the FIRST match found by walking that fixed order. In a directory containing both `package.json` and `composer.json`, `package.json` (node) always wins — there's no way to prioritize or reorder.

**Decided approach (do not relitigate):** Add a new segment option `priority` — a string array of project type names (matching the existing `.Type`/name strings already used for `<name>_files` overrides, e.g. `"node"`, `"php"`, `"cargo"`, etc). When `priority` is set and non-empty:
- Reorder `n.projects` so the listed names come first, in the given order.
- Any type NOT mentioned in `priority` stays in the slice, appended after the listed ones, in its original relative default order (i.e. this is a partial reorder, not a filter — omitting a type does not disable it).
- Unrecognized/misspelled names in `priority` are silently ignored (no error, no log noise) — just don't match anything in the reorder.
- When `priority` is absent or empty, behavior is 100% unchanged from today (this is the empty-value default — must not regress).

## Files to touch

1. `src/segments/project.go` — read `Enabled()` in full first (roughly lines 75-165, exact lines may differ) to see exactly how `n.projects` is built and iterated, then add the reorder logic. Keep it small — a helper function that takes the existing ordered slice and a priority list and returns a reordered slice is enough; don't restructure anything else.
2. `src/segments/project_test.go` — add test case(s) covering: (a) a directory with two project files, no `priority` set → confirms today's default-order behavior is preserved, (b) same directory WITH `priority` set to promote the second type, confirms it now wins, (c) `priority` containing an unknown name doesn't break anything.
3. `website/docs/segments/system/project.mdx` — this doc already documents the `<name>_files` per-type override pattern (grep for it). Add a short section/table row documenting the new `priority` option: type `[]string`, default `[]`, one-sentence description, and a short example matching the style already used in that file.
4. Check `themes/schema.json` for whether project-segment options are enumerated there (grep for the project segment's other option names like one of the `_files` ones, or `type: project`). If they are, add `priority` there following the existing pattern for a string-array property. If project segment options are NOT individually schema'd (e.g. it's a loose `additionalProperties` object), skip this file — don't add schema structure that doesn't match the existing convention.

## Non-goals — do not touch

- Do not implement "show all matching project files" — that requires changing the render/template contract (`ProjectData`, `Template()`) and is explicitly out of scope for this task.
- Do not change the default project-type order itself.
- Do not touch any project type's file-detection logic (`hasProjectFile`, fetchers, etc).

## Verification (run from `src/`, must all pass with zero errors before you report done)

```bash
cd src
go build ./...
go test ./segments/... -run TestProject -v
modernize --fix "./..."
fieldalignment --fix "./..."
go mod tidy
gofmt -w .
golangci-lint run ./segments/...
```
Run `git diff` after the modernize/fieldalignment/gofmt steps and confirm nothing unexpected got rewritten outside the files you touched.

## Do NOT commit

Leave all changes as uncommitted modifications in the worktree. Do not run `git add` or `git commit` — the orchestrator (a separate process) will review and commit.

## Report back

List every file changed with a one-line description of the change, the exact verification commands you ran and their pass/fail result (paste any failures verbatim, don't summarize them away), and explicitly flag anything the spec didn't cover that you had to make a judgment call on.

input.description: Implement issue 7531 extends chaining
input.prompt:
You are the implementer for one pinned task in the oh-my-posh Go project (module root is `src/`, not repo root — run all go commands from `src/`). You're in an isolated git worktree. Follow the repo's `.claude/skills/golang/SKILL.md` conventions exactly (early returns, no `else`, table-driven tests, etc).

## Task: GitHub issue #7531 — multi-level `extends` chaining

**Context — read this carefully, it corrects a common misconception:** Multi-hop `extends` chaining (A extends B extends C) ALREADY WORKS in `Parse` (`src/config/load.go`, roughly lines 119-136 — a `for cfg.Extends != ""` loop that reads each base, merges, and continues). Do NOT re-implement chaining itself. There are exactly two real, narrow gaps to fix:

1. **No cycle detection.** If A extends B extends A, the loop never terminates (both files exist, so there's no read error to break out on) — it hangs forever. Fix: track visited resolved file paths (the loop already computes `cfg.Extends` as a resolved/absolute path via `resolvePath` around load.go:120 before reading — confirm the exact resolution call). Before reading a hop, check if its resolved path is already in a `visited` set seeded with the initial config file's own resolved path; if so, `log.Error(...)` with a clear "circular extends" message and `break` out of the loop (matching the existing failure convention already used elsewhere in this same function for other read errors — find those existing `log.Error` + `break` sites around load.go:124/132 and mirror that exact style, don't invent a new error type or return signature).

2. **Relative path anchoring is wrong for hop 2+.** `parentFolder := filepath.Dir(configFile)` is computed ONCE before the loop and reused for every hop's `resolvePath` call, meaning a relative `extends` value inside the SECOND file resolves against the FIRST file's directory, not its own. Fix: after successfully reading each hop (and confirming it's a local file path, not a URL — check how the existing code distinguishes URL vs local paths for `extends`, likely near where `resolvePath` is called), update `parentFolder` to that hop's own directory (`filepath.Dir` of its resolved path) before resolving the NEXT hop's `Extends` value.

## Files to touch

- `src/config/load.go` — both fixes live in/around the `Parse` function's extends loop. Read the whole function first before changing anything.
- `src/config/load_test.go` (or wherever config load tests live — check for an existing file, e.g. `config_test.go`) — add test cases: (a) a 3-level extends chain (config A extends B extends C, each contributing distinct fields) resolves correctly with all levels merged — this proves chaining already worked AND still works after your change; (b) a 2-cycle (A extends B extends A) does not hang and produces a clear error/log instead; (c) a relative extends path in the second-level file resolves against ITS OWN directory, not the first file's — you'll likely need to create a small nested test-fixture directory structure (e.g. `testdata/extends/...`) for this; check if similar fixture directories already exist for other config tests and follow that pattern.

## Non-goals — do not touch

- Do not change single-level extends behavior.
- Do not touch `src/config/merge.go`'s field-merge algorithm — it's already correct (it already preserves the next hop's `Extends` field via `nextExtends := cfg.Extends` before/after the generic merge; verify this yourself but don't refactor it unless you find it's actually broken, in which case STOP and report back rather than improvising a fix).
- Do not add a max-depth cap — cycle detection via the visited set is the intended safeguard, not a depth limit.
- Do not touch URL-based `extends` (https:// configs) — relative-path resolution inside a URL-sourced config remains unresolvable, that's pre-existing and out of scope.

## Verification (run from `src/`, must all pass with zero errors before you report done)

```bash
cd src
go build ./...
go test ./config/... -v
modernize --fix "./..."
fieldalignment --fix "./..."
go mod tidy
gofmt -w .
golangci-lint run ./config/...
```
Run `git diff` after the modernize/fieldalignment/gofmt steps and confirm nothing unexpected got rewritten outside the files you touched.

## Do NOT commit

Leave all changes as uncommitted modifications in the worktree. Do not run `git add` or `git commit` — the orchestrator (a separate process) will review and commit.

## Report back

List every file changed with a one-line description, the exact verification commands you ran and their pass/fail result (paste failures verbatim), and explicitly flag anything you had to make a judgment call on that the spec didn't cover — especially if your reading of `merge.go` or the existing error-handling convention differs from what's described above.

input.description: Implement issue 6334 path regex expand
input.prompt:
You are the implementer for one pinned task in the oh-my-posh Go project (module root is `src/`, not repo root — run all go commands from `src/`). You're in an isolated git worktree. Follow the repo's `.claude/skills/golang/SKILL.md` conventions exactly (early returns, no `else`, table-driven tests, etc).

## Task: GitHub issue #6334 — path segment, multiple/named capture groups in `mapped_locations`

**Problem:** In `src/segments/path.go`, the `replaceMappedLocations` function's `handleRegex` closure (roughly lines 703-726) calls `regex.FindStringMatch(pattern, input, 1)` (`src/regex/regex.go`, `FindStringMatch`, roughly lines 113-130) with a HARDCODED index of 1 — meaning only capture group 1's matched text is available, and it's substituted into the input via a plain `strings.Replace(input, match, pt.mappedLocations[key], 1)`. There's no way for a `mapped_locations` regex value to reference `$1`, `$2`, or named groups like `${repo}`.

**Decided approach (do not relitigate):** Add a new PER-SEGMENT boolean option `mapped_locations_regex_expand` (default `false`, read the same way `MappedLocationsEnabled` is read via `pt.options.Bool(...)` near path.go:101-104 — find that exact pattern and mirror it). When this option is `true`, for any `mapped_locations` key with the `re:` prefix, `handleRegex` must:
1. Confirm the pattern matches the input (do NOT extract just group 1).
2. Use the existing `regex.ReplaceAllString` helper (`src/regex/regex.go`, roughly lines 95-102 — check it exists with that exact signature first) to replace the FULL regex match in `input`, expanding `$1`, `${name}`, etc. from `pt.mappedLocations[key]` per Go's standard `regexp.Expand` semantics.
3. When the option is `false` (the default), behavior is 100% unchanged — same hardcoded-group-1 + `strings.Replace` path as today. This preserves default behavior exactly, including its known quirks — do not "fix" the legacy path's behavior in this task.
4. The `(?i)` Windows/WSL case-insensitivity prefix logic (path.go around lines 711-714) must apply identically regardless of which mode is active — it prepends to `pattern` before either matching function runs, so this should compose without special-casing, but verify it in your implementation.

**A separate, real bug exists in the legacy (default, `mapped_locations_regex_expand=false`) code path:** `strings.Replace(input, match, value, 1)` replaces the FIRST TEXTUAL OCCURRENCE of the captured substring anywhere in `input`, not the occurrence at the actual regex match position — so if the same substring appears twice (e.g. pattern captures the second `src` in `/home/src/project/src/deep`), the wrong occurrence gets replaced. **Do NOT fix this in this task** — it's default-behavior-changing and needs a separate decision. Just note it in your final report as confirmed-but-intentionally-untouched.

## Files to touch

1. `src/segments/path.go` — read `replaceMappedLocations` and `handleRegex` in full first, then add the new option and branch.
2. `src/segments/path_test.go` — add test cases: (a) existing group-1-only behavior unchanged when the option is unset/false, (b) `mapped_locations_regex_expand: true` with a pattern using `${name}` named-group expansion producing the expected output, (c) same with positional `$1`/`$2`, (d) confirm the option composes correctly with the Windows case-insensitive path (if there's an existing test helper/flag for that, use it).
3. `themes/schema.json` — grep for where the path segment's other boolean options (e.g. `mapped_locations_enabled` or similar — check the exact existing key name) are declared in the schema, and add `mapped_locations_regex_expand` following the exact same pattern (type boolean, description, default false).
4. Path segment docs — find the doc file (likely `website/docs/segments/system/path.mdx` or similar — grep for where `mapped_locations` is currently documented) and add a short section for the new option with a concrete before/after example (e.g. the git-worktree-folder-name use case from the original issue: `re:(.*/GitHub/(?P<repo>.*)\.worktrees/.*)` → `/${repo}` — pick a real icon codepoint already used elsewhere in that doc file for style consistency, or use plain text if unsure).

## Non-goals — do not touch

- Do not change default (option-off) behavior, including the known first-occurrence bug described above.
- Do not implement the issue reporter's option 1 (positional multi-group replace) or option 3 (sequential all-mappings iteration, from issue #6166) — only option 2 (this expand toggle).
- Do not touch unrelated mapped_locations logic (non-regex literal-key matching).

## Verification (run from `src/`, must all pass with zero errors before you report done)

```bash
cd src
go build ./...
go test ./segments/... -run TestPath -v
modernize --fix "./..."
fieldalignment --fix "./..."
go mod tidy
gofmt -w .
golangci-lint run ./segments/...
```
Run `git diff` after the modernize/fieldalignment/gofmt steps and confirm nothing unexpected got rewritten outside the files you touched.

## Do NOT commit

Leave all changes as uncommitted modifications in the worktree. Do not run `git add` or `git commit` — the orchestrator (a separate process) will review and commit.

## Report back

List every file changed with a one-line description, the exact verification commands you ran and their pass/fail result (paste failures verbatim), and explicitly flag anything you had to make a judgment call on.

input.description: Implement issue 5442 restart_cycle
input.prompt:
You are the implementer for one pinned task in the oh-my-posh Go project (module root is `src/`, not repo root — run all go commands from `src/`). You're in an isolated git worktree. Follow the repo's `.claude/skills/golang/SKILL.md` conventions exactly (early returns, no `else`, table-driven tests, etc).

## Task: GitHub issue #5442 — per-block color cycle restart

**Problem:** The color cycle (`color.Cycle`, `src/color/cycle.go`, a `[]*Set` with a `Loop()` method that rotates and returns the head — no explicit index, it mutates the slice order itself) is ONE shared, package-level, mutable pointer (`var cycle *color.Cycle`, `src/prompt/engine.go` — NOT segments.go, verify the exact line) set once per prompt render to `&e.Config.Cycle` (`src/prompt/primary.go`, around line 64). Every rendered segment across EVERY block consumes and advances it unconditionally, at two call sites: `writeSegment` in `src/prompt/segments.go` (roughly lines 191-195, the live/foreground render path) and `renderBlockFromCache` in `src/prompt/engine.go` (roughly lines 316-320, the cached/streaming path). Because it's shared across blocks in render order, a second block (e.g. a two-line prompt's second line) starts wherever the first block's segment count left the rotation — not at the cycle's first color.

**Decided approach (do not relitigate):** Add a new boolean field `RestartCycle` to the `Block` struct (`src/config/block.go`, roughly lines 30-41 — check the exact struct and existing field/tag style to match it precisely; json/yaml/toml tag should be `restart_cycle`). At the top of BOTH block-render entry points — find `renderBlockSegments` (`src/prompt/segments.go`, roughly line 69 — confirm this is really the single choke point that both the primary/rprompt/tooltip live-render paths funnel through) AND `renderBlockFromCache` (`src/prompt/engine.go`, roughly line 303) — add: `if block.RestartCycle { cycle = &e.Config.Cycle }` (adjust exact variable names/receiver to match what you find in the real code) BEFORE that block's segments are rendered. This re-points the shared global back at the pristine, unmutated config cycle, so the block's own segments (and everything rendered after it, until another restart or end of prompt) start from the cycle's first color again.

Verify `Loop()` truly never mutates `e.Config.Cycle` itself (only returns a rotated copy/view) — this is what makes "re-point at `&e.Config.Cycle`" a safe, correct reset. If you find `Loop()` DOES mutate the underlying config value in place, STOP and report back — the whole approach needs rethinking and that's a decision for the orchestrator, not something to improvise around.

## Files to touch

1. `src/config/block.go` — add the `RestartCycle` field.
2. `src/prompt/segments.go` and `src/prompt/engine.go` — add the reset check at both render entry points.
3. `src/prompt/segments_test.go` and/or `src/prompt/engine_test.go` (check which file already has block-rendering tests) — add a test: render a 2-block prompt where block 1's segments consume N cycle colors, block 2 has `RestartCycle: true`, confirm block 2's first segment gets the cycle's first color (not the N+1th). Also a control case without the flag confirming today's carry-over behavior is unchanged.
4. `themes/schema.json` — grep for where other `Block`-level boolean options are declared (e.g. search for an existing block property in the schema) and add `restart_cycle` following the same pattern.
5. Block config docs — find the doc file documenting block-level properties (likely `website/docs/configuration/block.mdx` or similar — grep for where existing block properties like `alignment` or `type` are documented) and add `restart_cycle` with a short description and example (e.g. a two-line prompt where the second line always starts the cycle fresh).

## Non-goals — do not touch

- Do not change `Cycle.Loop()`'s rotation algorithm.
- Do not implement per-segment cycle control or cycle exclusion (skip-entirely) — only the block-level restart flag.
- Do not touch transient/secondary/debug prompt rendering unless those paths also funnel through the same two render entry points you're modifying (in which case the fix applies naturally — just don't go add NEW cycle-consumption logic to paths that don't already have it).

## Verification (run from `src/`, must all pass with zero errors before you report done)

```bash
cd src
go build ./...
go test ./prompt/... ./config/... -v
modernize --fix "./..."
fieldalignment --fix "./..."
go mod tidy
gofmt -w .
golangci-lint run ./prompt/... ./config/...
```
Run `git diff` after the modernize/fieldalignment/gofmt steps and confirm nothing unexpected got rewritten outside the files you touched.

## Do NOT commit

Leave all changes as uncommitted modifications in the worktree. Do not run `git add` or `git commit` — the orchestrator (a separate process) will review and commit.

## Report back

List every file changed with a one-line description, the exact verification commands you ran and their pass/fail result (paste failures verbatim), and explicitly flag anything you had to make a judgment call on — especially the `Loop()` mutation question above.

## Summary

**Files changed:**

1. **`src/segments/path.go`** — Added `MappedLocationsRegexExpand options.Option = "mapped_locations_regex_expand"` constant (mirrors `MappedLocationsEnabled`), and branched `handleRegex` in `replaceMappedLocations`: when `pt.options.Bool(MappedLocationsRegexExpand, false)` is true, it uses `regex.MatchString` to confirm a match then `regex.ReplaceAllString(pattern, input, pt.mappedLocations[key])` to replace the full match with an expanded value (`$1`, `${name}`, etc.). The `(?i)` Windows/WSL prefix logic is untouched and applies to `pattern` before either branch runs, so it composes automatically. Default path (option unset/false) is byte-for-byte unchanged — same `regex.FindStringMatch(pattern, input, 1)` + `strings.Replace` logic as before.

2. **`src/segments/path_test.go`** — Added `MappedLocationsRegexExpand bool` field to `testFullPathCustomMappedLocationsCase` and wired it into the `props` map in `TestFullPathCustomMappedLocations`.

3. **`src/segments/path_unix_test.go`** — Added 3 cases: (a) option unset — a `$1` in the value stays literal (proves legacy behavior unchanged), (b) `MappedLocationsRegexExpand: true` with named group `${repo}` (the issue's git-worktree use case), (c) same with positional `$1`/`$2`.

4. **`src/segments/path_windows_test.go`** — Added 1 case confirming `MappedLocationsRegexExpand: true` composes correctly with the Windows `(?i)` case-insensitivity prefix (pattern uses lowercase `github`, actual path has `GitHub`).

5. **`themes/schema.json`** — Added `mapped_locations_regex_expand` boolean property (type/title/description/default false) following the exact pattern of `mapped_locations_enabled`.

6. **`website/docs/segments/system/path.mdx`** — Added a `mapped_locations_regex_expand` row to the Mapped Locations options table, and a new "Expanding capture groups" subsection with a before/after example using the GitHub icon (`\uF09B`, already used earlier in the file) and the exact worktree-folder-name use case from issue #6334.

**Verification commands run (all from `src/`), all passed:**
```

golangci-lint run ./segments/...                         → 0 issues
go test ./segments/...                                   → ok (both segments and segments/options)
```
`git status --porcelain` confirms only the 6 intended files are modified.

**Judgment calls / things to flag:**

- **Self-inflicted near-miss, caught and fixed:** while iterating on markdownlint fixes, I ran `git stash` to compare baseline lint output and immediately `git stash pop`'d to restore — no data lost, but worth noting. Separately, `npx markdownlint-cli2 --fix` corrupted content twice: it rewrote `.*` to `._` inside the regex example (mangling the actual regex) and stripped table padding to zero, and a follow-up regex-based repair script I wrote was too greedy and deleted the entire original 20-row "Options" table plus the "## Mapped Locations" heading/intro. I caught this immediately via `git diff`/`Read`, recovered the exact original text via `git show HEAD:...`, and reconstructed the file surgically. Final `git diff` on the doc file is clean and minimal — confirmed by pasting it above.
- **`regex.ReplaceAllString` replaces all non-overlapping matches**, not just the first — this is standard Go `regexp.ReplaceAllString` semantics and is what the task explicitly designated as the helper to use, but it's worth knowing this differs from `FindStringMatch`'s single-match semantics in the legacy path.
- **`-run TestPath` matches nothing** in this codebase — no test function is literally named with that as a substring (they're `TestFullPathCustomMappedLocations`, `TestAgnosterPathStyles`, etc.). I ran the actual set of path-related test functions instead and confirmed all pass, including the new cases.

Left exactly as specified — out of scope for this task.

+### Expanding capture groups
+
+By default, only the first capture group is used, and its matched text is replaced as-is; additional groups, whether
+positional or named, are ignored. Set `mapped_locations_regex_expand` to `true` to reference capture groups in the
+replacement value instead, using `$1`, `$2`, or `${name}` for named groups. When enabled, the full regex match (not
+just the first capture group) is replaced with the expanded value.
+
+For example, to display the repository name instead of the Git worktree folder, you can capture it in a named group
+and reference it in the replacement:
+
+<!-- markdownlint-disable MD033 MD049 -->
+
+<Config
+  data={{
+    options: {
+      mapped_locations_regex_expand: true,
+      mapped_locations: {
+        "re:(.*/GitHub/(?P<repo>.*)\\.worktrees/.*)": "\uF09B ${repo}",
+      },
+    },
+  }}
+/>
+
+<!-- markdownlint-enable MD033 MD049 -->
+
+This turns `~/GitHub/oh-my-posh.worktrees/feature-branch` into the GitHub icon followed by `oh-my-posh`.

79	### How it works
80	
81	- To make mapped locations work cross-platform, use `/` as the path separator, Oh My Posh will
82	  automatically match effective separators based on the running operating system.
83	- If you want to match all child directories, you can use `*` as a wildcard, for example:
84	  `"C:/Users/Bill/*": "$"` will turn `C:/Users/Bill/Downloads` into `$/Downloads` but leave `C:/Users/Bill` unchanged.
85	- The character `~` at the start of a mapped location will match the user's home directory.
86	- The match is _case-insensitive on Windows and macOS_, but case-sensitive on other operating systems. This means that for
87	  user Bill, who has a user account `Bill` on Windows and `bill` on Linux, `~/Foo` might match
88	  `C:\Users\Bill\Foo` or `C:\Users\Bill\foo` on Windows but only `/home/bill/Foo` on Linux.
89	
90	:::warning
91	To prevent mangling path elements, if you use any text style tags (e.g., `<lightGreen>...</>`) in replacement values,
92	you should avoid using a chevron character (`<`/`>`) in the `folder_separator_icon` property, and vice versa.
93	:::
94	
95	### Using regular expressions
96	
97	For more complicated cases, you can use the `re:` prefix to use a regular expression with a capture group for matching.
98	This uses Golang's [regexp] package, so you can use any of the [supported syntax][regexp]. The replacement value will be the first capture group,
99	subsequent groups will be ignored.
100	
101	For example, `"re:(C:/[0-9]+/Foo)": "#"` will match `C:\123\Foo\Bar` and replace it with `#\Bar`. The path used for matching
102	will always use `/`, regardless of the operating system, allowing cross platform matching.

31	// launchBlockSegments starts execution for every segment in the block and
32	// returns the channel that will receive their results as they complete.
33	// Callers may consume the channel immediately or defer consumption to
34	// allow other blocks' segments to execute concurrently in the meantime.
35	// Returns nil when the block has no segments.
36	func (e *Engine) launchBlockSegments(block *config.Block) chan result {
37		length := len(block.Segments)
38	
39		if length == 0 {
40			return nil
41		}
42	
43		out := make(chan result, length)
44	
45		e.writeSegmentsConcurrently(block.Segments, out)
46	
47		return out
48	}
49

51	// in executed. Calling this for every block before any rendering begins ensures
52	// the executed map is fully populated, so cross-block .Segments.X dependencies
53	// resolve in both directions (an earlier block can reference a later block's
54	// segment and vice versa).
55	func drainBlockResults(out chan result, count int, executed map[string]bool) []*config.Segment {
56		results := make([]*config.Segment, count)
57		for range count {
58			res := <-out
59			results[res.index] = res.segment
60			executed[res.segment.Name()] = true
61		}

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\conventional-commit

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

## Delivered

Four commits on `next`, one per issue, all backward compatible by default:

| Commit | Issue | What |
|---|---|---|
| [`3c77bed1`](src/segments/project.go) | [#7594](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7594) | `priority` option on the project segment — reorders which project file wins when a directory has more than one |
| [`7134637d`](src/config/load.go) | [#7531](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7531) | Extends chaining already worked; fixed the hang on a circular chain and the wrong-directory relative-path bug on hop 2+ |
| [`982337b8`](src/segments/path.go) | [#6334](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6334) | `mapped_locations_regex_expand` opt-in on the path segment — lets a `re:` value reference `$1`/`${name}` against the full match, not just capture group 1 |
| [`ebcac0c1`](src/config/block.go) | [#5442](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/5442) | `restart_cycle` on `Block` — resets the shared color cycle to its first color before that block renders |

**Where I overrode/adjusted implementer output:** fixed one table-formatting inconsistency in [project.mdx](website/docs/segments/system/project.mdx) (implementer wrote `[]string` for the `priority` type while the sibling row used `array`); split the shared `themes/schema.json` diff into per-commit hunks by hand since three tasks touched that one file in different, non-adjacent locations.

**Verification, run independently on the final merged state** (not per-task): `go build ./...` and cross-compiled `GOOS=linux` — clean; full `go test ./...` — all packages pass, including the new tests for cycle-hang, per-hop relative paths, priority reordering, capture-group expansion, and cycle restart; `modernize`, `fieldalignment`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt` — zero changes needed; `golangci-lint run ./...` — 0 issues. Doc lint: `path.mdx` and `block.mdx` introduced zero new violations; `project.mdx` picked up exactly one new pre-existing-style line-length warning from the new table row (that file already carries 16 such violations — not a regression I chased, since fixing them means reformatting an unrelated table).

**Loose ends left for you:**
- A real, unrelated bug was found and deliberately **not** fixed: `src/config/merge.go`'s `isZeroValue` treats an unset bool/int field the same as an explicit `false`/`0`, so `merge()` can silently clobber a base config's bool/int value with a hop that never set it. Worth its own issue.
- Also confirmed-but-untouched: the legacy (non-expand) `mapped_locations` regex path replaces the *first textual occurrence* of the captured substring, not the occurrence at the match position — a latent bug independent of #6334, left alone since fixing it changes default behavior.
- Nothing pushed — commits are local to your `next` branch only, per your no-push-unless-asked default.
You can fix the two bugs as well here.

input.description: Analyze merge.go zero-value bug
input.prompt:
You are the orchestrator (analysis phase) for a Go CLI project, oh-my-posh, at D:\oh-my-posh (prompt theme engine, module root is `src/`). You're following this project's "code-changes" skill workflow, Phase 1 (Analyze). Do NOT write or edit any code in this phase — read-only investigation only.

## The bug

A prior implementer working on an unrelated `extends`-chaining task discovered and flagged, but did not fix, this issue in `src/config/merge.go`:

> `merge()`'s `isZeroValue` treats `bool` and numeric-kind fields as ALWAYS returning `false` (i.e. "not a zero value" is never true, or "is zero value" logic differs for these kinds vs strings/structs), meaning `merge()` unconditionally overwrites a base config's bool/int field with the override's value even when the override's source file never actually set that field (so the override's parsed Go value is just the Go zero value: `false`/`0`). Concretely: if config A extends config B, and B sets some bool field to `true`, but A's JSON/YAML/TOML never mentions that field at all, after Parse+merge the final config ends up with the field back at `false` — A's *absence* of the field silently clobbers B's explicit `true`.

This was worked around in a test by using only string-typed fields, not fixed.

## Your task

1. Read `src/config/merge.go` in full — especially the `merge()` function and its `isZeroValue` helper (or whatever it's actually named/shaped now; verify against the real code, don't trust the description above blindly regarding exact names).
2. Confirm the bug is real: trace exactly what `isZeroValue` returns for a `bool` field that's `false` (both "explicitly set to false in source" and "never mentioned in source" cases) and for an `int`/numeric field that's `0`, versus what it returns for an empty string or nil slice/map. Is there really no way today to distinguish "the override didn't set this field" from "the override explicitly set this field to the zero value"?
3. Figure out how configs get from raw JSON/YAML/TOML text into the `Config` struct in the first place — find the unmarshal/decode call(s) (likely in `src/config/load.go` or a `read`/`Default`-adjacent function). Does that decode step have ANY access to "which keys were present in the source" (e.g. does it decode into `map[string]interface{}` first, or use `json.RawMessage`, or a library that tracks field-presence), or is that information already thrown away by the time `merge()` runs on two fully-typed `*Config` structs?
4. This determines the fix's actual difficulty. Consider and evaluate, in order of increasing invasiveness, and recommend ONE:
   a. **If presence info is fully gone by merge time**: the honest fix requires decoding each config into a generic presence-tracking structure (e.g. `map[string]interface{}`) BEFORE merge, and only merging keys that were actually present in the override's JSON/YAML/TOML — this could be a real, non-trivial architectural change touching how configs are parsed. Describe concretely what changes and how big it is (is there already a generic-map decode step happening anywhere nearby you could reuse, e.g. for the JSON schema validation?).
   b. **If some lighter-weight signal already exists or is feasible** (e.g. every affected field could be converted to a pointer type — `*bool`/`*int` — so the zero value becomes `nil` and merge already special-cases nil correctly): describe exactly which fields are affected across `Config`/`Block`/`Segment` and whether pointer-ifying them is remotely proportionate, or whether it would ripple through every reader of those fields across the codebase (search for a few field usages to estimate blast radius, e.g. grep for `.Force`, `.Newline`, or similar directly-used bool fields elsewhere in `src/`).
   c. **A narrow, honest partial fix**: e.g. only fix it for whichever specific fields are actually causing real user-facing bugs today (if any known ones), leaving the general `isZeroValue` semantics alone, with a clear comment documenting the known limitation.
5. Give a very clear feasibility verdict: is this a "small contained fix" or "this is actually a substantial refactor and here's why" — be honest, don't undersell the scope just because a small fix would be nicer to hear.

## Output format (exact — will be pasted into a user-facing report)

```
### Bug — merge.go isZeroValue clobbers unset bool/int fields

**Root cause:** ...(file:line, exact mechanism)...

**Blast radius:** ...(how many fields/structs affected, what decode step exists today)...

**Recommended fix:** ...(concrete: option a/b/c above, what changes, what file(s), roughly how many lines/how invasive)...

**Feasibility verdict:** small / moderate / substantial-refactor — with the specific reason.

**Open questions:** ...
```

Keep the whole response under 450 words — this one matters more than usual, so use the budget on the blast-radius and feasibility sections, not restating the bug description.

input.description: Analyze path.go replace occurrence bug
input.prompt:
You are the orchestrator (analysis phase) for a Go CLI project, oh-my-posh, at D:\oh-my-posh (prompt theme engine, module root is `src/`). You're following this project's "code-changes" skill workflow, Phase 1 (Analyze). Do NOT write or edit any code in this phase — read-only investigation only.

## The bug

In `src/segments/path.go`, function `replaceMappedLocations`'s `handleRegex` closure, the DEFAULT (legacy, `mapped_locations_regex_expand` option unset/false) code path does roughly:

```go
match, OK := regex.FindStringMatch(pattern, input, 1)  // returns matches[1] (capture group 1's TEXT, no position info)
...
input = strings.Replace(input, match, pt.mappedLocations[key], 1)  // replaces the FIRST TEXTUAL OCCURRENCE of that text anywhere in input
input = path.Clean(input)
```

The bug: `strings.Replace` finds and replaces the first occurrence of the captured substring's TEXT anywhere in `input`, not the occurrence at the actual regex match's position. Example: pattern `re:.*/(src)/deep` matched against `/home/src/project/src/deep` — capture group 1 is the SECOND `src` (the one right before `/deep`), but `strings.Replace(input, "src", value, 1)` rewrites the FIRST `src` in the string (`/home/src/...`), which is the wrong one.

There's a sibling `mapped_locations_regex_expand=true` code path (added recently, already correct — it uses `regex.ReplaceAllString` which operates on the actual regex match, not a separate text-search-and-replace) that does NOT have this bug. Only the legacy/default path is affected.

## Your task

1. Read `src/segments/path.go`'s `replaceMappedLocations` / `handleRegex` in full (current state — a recent commit already added the `mapped_locations_regex_expand` branch, so read the CURRENT file, not an old version) to confirm the exact mechanism and current line numbers.
2. Confirm the bug is real and reproducible in principle: construct one or two concrete example paths + regex patterns where the current code would produce a demonstrably wrong result (be specific about input, pattern, expected-vs-actual output).
3. Design a minimal, position-aware fix for the DEFAULT path specifically. Options to consider: (a) use `regexp.Regexp.FindStringSubmatchIndex` to get the byte offsets of capture group 1, then do a position-based splice (`input[:start] + value + input[end:]`) instead of `strings.Replace`; (b) reuse the existing `regex.ReplaceAllString` helper (already used by the expand path) with an `Expand`-style template that's just `$1` — but check whether that changes behavior when the SAME overall pattern matches multiple times in the input (currently `strings.Replace`'s count=1 vs `ReplaceAllString`'s replace-all-matches could differ; note this explicitly). Recommend the option that most narrowly fixes ONLY the positional bug without silently changing other behavior (e.g. multiple-match handling) that isn't part of this bug report.
4. This is fixing DEFAULT behavior (not behind a flag) — flag explicitly whether you think this is safe to ship as a plain bug fix (i.e., is there any remotely plausible existing config that depends on the CURRENT wrong-occurrence behavior on purpose)? Give your honest judgment.
5. Note what test case(s) would prove the fix (the exact repro from step 2, both Windows and Unix path variants if relevant — check if there are already separate `path_unix_test.go`/`path_windows_test.go` files with parallel structures for the existing `mapped_locations` regex tests).

## Output format (exact — will be pasted into a user-facing report)

```
### Bug — path.go legacy mapped_locations regex replaces wrong occurrence

**Root cause:** ...(file:line, exact mechanism)...

**Concrete repro:** ...(input path, pattern, current wrong output, correct expected output)...

**Recommended fix:** ...(concrete: which option, what file/lines change)...

**Safety of fixing default behavior:** ...(your honest judgment)...

**Open questions:** ...
```

Keep the whole response under 350 words.

<result>I've confirmed everything. Analysis complete.

### Bug — path.go legacy mapped_locations regex replaces wrong occurrence

**Root cause:** `D:\oh-my-posh\src\segments\path.go:732-738` (`handleRegex`, default path). `regex.FindStringMatch(pattern, input, 1)` (`src/regex/regex.go:113-130`) returns only the *text* of capture group 1 via `FindStringSubmatch` — no position. Line 738 then does `strings.Replace(input, match, value, 1)`, which rewrites the first *textual* occurrence of that substring anywhere in the path, not the occurrence the regex actually captured.

**Concrete repro:** Pwd `/home/src/project/src/deep`, mapped location `re:.*/(src)/deep` → `#`. Greedy `.*` makes group 1 the second `src`. Current output: `/home/#/project/src/deep` (first `src` replaced). Expected: `/home/src/project/#/deep`. Same shape: `/b/x/b/c` with `re:.*(/b)/c` → `#` gives `#/x/b/c` instead of `/b/x/#/c`.

**Recommended fix:** Option (a), position-based splice. Add a small helper in `src/regex/regex.go` (e.g. `FindStringMatchIndex(pattern, text, index) (start, end int, bool)` wrapping `re.FindStringSubmatchIndex`, offsets `[2*index]`,`[2*index+1]`), then in `path.go:732-738` replace the `strings.Replace` with `input[:start] + pt.mappedLocations[key] + input[end:]`. Option (b) (`regex.ReplaceAllString` with a template) is ruled out on two counts: it replaces the *full* match rather than only group 1's span, and it expands `$`-references in the mapped value — the existing legacy test (`src/segments/path_unix_test.go:645-649`) explicitly asserts `#$1` stays literal. It also replaces all matches vs. count=1. Option (a) changes only the position, nothing else.

**Safety of fixing default behavior:** Safe. Depending on the current behavior requires the captured text to appear verbatim *earlier* in the path than where it was captured — and the config author to want the earlier copy rewritten. That's pathological; typical patterns anchor from the string start, where both behaviors coincide. Ship as a plain fix.

**Open questions:** None blocking. Test coverage: add the repro above to `testFullPathCustomMappedLocationsCases` in `path_unix_test.go` (~line 649) and a backslash-input sibling in `path_windows_test.go` (~line 436) — parallel structures already exist for regex mapped locations.</result>

<result>All investigation done. Here is the report.

### Bug — merge.go isZeroValue clobbers unset bool/int fields

**Root cause:** `src/config/merge.go:166-179` — `isZeroValue` returns `false` unconditionally for `reflect.Bool` and signed-int kinds (lines 174-175), so the skip at line 137 (`if isZeroValue(overrideField) ... continue`) never fires for them and `merge()` always copies the override's value. Since `read()` (`src/config/load.go:195-254`) unmarshals each file straight into a typed `Config` via `yaml.Unmarshal`/`json.Decoder`/`toml.Unmarshal`, "field absent" and "field explicitly false/0" are identical by merge time — presence info is fully discarded. Confirmed: child extending a base with `final_space: true` but omitting `final_space` yields `false`. `merge_test.go:20-41` encodes this clobbering as *expected*. Bonus inconsistency: uint/float fall into the `default: v.IsZero()` branch, so they have the opposite bug (explicit `0` override is ignored).

**Blast radius:** 20 source-settable fields: Config — `Async`, `ShellIntegration`, `FinalSpace`, `PatchPwshBleed`, `EnableCursorPositioning`, `Version`, `Streaming`; Block — `Newline`, `Force`, `RestartCycle`, `Index`; Segment — `Newline`, `InvertPowerline`, `Force`, `Toggled`, `Interactive`, `Index`, `MinWidth`, `MaxWidth`, `Timeout`. Pointer-ifying them (option b) touches ~60+ read sites across `src/` plus gob caching, defaults, DSC, the JSON schema, and docs — disproportionate. No presence signal exists in the config path today, but `src/config/data.go` already has the exact generic-decode machinery needed (`normalize`/`sanitize` convert YAML/TOML/JSON uniformly to generic maps); custom unmarshalers are not an option because go-toml/v2 doesn't support them (noted at `load.go:155-156`).

**Recommended fix:** Option a, scoped. In `read()`, decode the raw bytes a second time into a generic map (reusing `data.go`'s `sanitize` pattern) and record key-presence sets in unexported fields on `Config`, `Block`, and `Segment` (arrays pair positionally with the parsed slices — all three decoders preserve order). In `merge()`, for scalar kinds consult the override's presence set (via the field's `json` tag) instead of the bool/int `false` special case; nil presence set falls back to current semantics so direct struct-level callers/tests keep working. Files: `merge.go`, `load.go`, small additions to `config.go`/`block.go`/`segment.go`; roughly 150-250 lines plus test updates (including flipping the expectation in `merge_test.go:20-41`).

**Feasibility verdict:** moderate — not a contained one-liner because presence must be captured at decode time and threaded through the block/segment matching in `Config.merge`, but it reuses existing generic-decode helpers and stays inside `src/config` with no ripple into field readers.

**Open questions:** (1) Fix strings/uints uniformly (explicit `""`/`0` overrides would start working — a behavior change) or bool/int only? (2) Should `Version` be exempt (always present in practice)? (3) Presence for the deprecated `properties`→`options` TOML migration path needs a look during implementation.</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>61624</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>12</tool_uses><duration_ms>124241</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

Both analyses are in. The merge.go bug is real, and rated **moderate** — not a one-liner, but contained inside `src/config` since it can reuse existing generic-decode machinery already in `data.go`. The path.go fix is small and safe. I'm pinning the design decisions now and delegating both to Sonnet 5 implementers.

input.description: Fix path.go wrong-occurrence bug
input.prompt:
You are the implementer for one pinned bug-fix task in the oh-my-posh Go project (module root is `src/`, not repo root — run all go commands from `src/`). You're in an isolated git worktree. Follow the repo's `.claude/skills/golang/SKILL.md` conventions exactly (early returns, no `else`, table-driven tests, etc).

## Bug: legacy `mapped_locations` regex replaces the wrong occurrence

In `src/segments/path.go`, `replaceMappedLocations`'s `handleRegex` closure has a DEFAULT (legacy, `mapped_locations_regex_expand` option unset/false) branch that does roughly:

```go
match, OK := regex.FindStringMatch(pattern, input, 1)  // regex.go: returns matches[1] TEXT only, no position
...
input = strings.Replace(input, match, pt.mappedLocations[key], 1)  // replaces FIRST TEXTUAL occurrence anywhere in input
input = path.Clean(input)
```

**The bug:** `strings.Replace` finds and replaces the first occurrence of the captured substring's TEXT anywhere in `input`, not the occurrence at the actual regex match's position. Confirmed repro: `Pwd` = `/home/src/project/src/deep`, mapped location `re:.*/(src)/deep` → `#`. Greedy `.*` makes capture group 1 the SECOND `src` (right before `/deep`). Current (wrong) output: `/home/#/project/src/deep`. Correct expected output: `/home/src/project/#/deep`.

There is a sibling `mapped_locations_regex_expand=true` branch (added in a recent commit) that does NOT have this bug — it uses `regex.ReplaceAllString`, which operates on the actual match, not text search. That branch is correct; leave it alone.

## Decided fix (do not relitigate)

Position-based splice using submatch byte offsets, scoped ONLY to the legacy/default branch:

1. In `src/regex/regex.go`, add a new function alongside the existing `FindStringMatch` (currently ~line 113-130) and `MatchString`/`ReplaceAllString` (~line 95-112): something like `FindStringMatchIndex(pattern, text string, index int) (start, end int, ok bool)` that compiles the pattern (reuse the same compiled-regex-cache mechanism `FindStringMatch` already uses — check exactly how `GetCompiledRegex` works and mirror it), calls `re.FindStringSubmatchIndex(text)`, and returns `matches[2*index]`, `matches[2*index+1]`, true when the match exists and the capture group actually participated (mirror `FindStringMatch`'s existing `len(matches) <= index` and `len(match) == 0`-style guards, adapted for the index-pair form — note `FindStringSubmatchIndex` returns `-1, -1` for a capture group that didn't participate, so check for that specifically, not just slice length).
2. In `src/segments/path.go`'s `handleRegex`, in the legacy/default branch only, replace the `regex.FindStringMatch` + `strings.Replace` pair with: call your new `regex.FindStringMatchIndex(pattern, input, 1)`, and on success do `input = input[:start] + pt.mappedLocations[key] + input[end:]` instead of `strings.Replace(...)`. Keep everything else in that branch (the `path.Clean(input)` call, the `return input, true` shape) unchanged.
3. Do NOT touch the `mapped_locations_regex_expand=true` branch — it's already correct and uses a different helper (`regex.ReplaceAllString`).
4. Do NOT change any other behavior: this is a pure positional-correctness fix. The value substituted, the "only group 1, first match" semantics, and everything else about the legacy path's observable behavior for the COMMON case (single occurrence) must stay identical — only the wrong-occurrence-when-duplicated-text case changes.

## Files to touch

1. `src/regex/regex.go` — add `FindStringMatchIndex`.
2. `src/regex/regex_test.go` (if it exists — check) — add a direct unit test for the new function: a pattern where the captured text appears twice in the input, confirming the returned indices point at the SECOND (actually-captured) occurrence, not the first.
3. `src/segments/path.go` — swap the legacy branch's matching/replace logic as described.
4. `src/segments/path_unix_test.go` — add the confirmed repro case to `testFullPathCustomMappedLocationsCases` (there's already a parallel structure there for `mapped_locations_regex_expand` cases from a recent commit — follow that exact style): `Pwd: "/home/src/project/src/deep"`, `MappedLocations: map[string]string{"re:.*/(src)/deep": "#"}`, `Expected: "/home/src/project/#/deep"` (verify the exact expected string format matches how other cases in that file express expected output — check for leading-slash/trailing conventions).
5. `src/segments/path_windows_test.go` — add a backslash-input sibling case following the existing parallel-structure convention in that file.

## Non-goals — do not touch

- Do not touch the `mapped_locations_regex_expand=true` branch.
- Do not change multi-match semantics (this stays first-match-only, `count=1` equivalent — a single splice, not `ReplaceAll`).
- Do not touch non-regex (literal-key) `mapped_locations` matching.

## Verification (run from `src/`, must all pass with zero errors before you report done)

```bash
cd src
go build ./...
go test ./regex/... ./segments/... -v
modernize --fix "./..."
fieldalignment --fix "./..."
go mod tidy
gofmt -w .
golangci-lint run ./regex/... ./segments/...
```
Run `git diff` after the modernize/fieldalignment/gofmt steps and confirm nothing unexpected got rewritten outside the files you touched.

## Do NOT commit

Leave all changes as uncommitted modifications in the worktree. Do not run `git add` or `git commit` — the orchestrator (a separate process) will review and commit.

## Report back

List every file changed with a one-line description, the exact verification commands you ran and their pass/fail result (paste failures verbatim), and explicitly flag anything you had to make a judgment call on.

input.description: Fix merge.go presence-tracking bug
input.prompt:
You are the implementer for one pinned bug-fix task in the oh-my-posh Go project (module root is `src/`, not repo root — run all go commands from `src/`). You're in an isolated git worktree. Follow the repo's `.claude/skills/golang/SKILL.md` conventions exactly (early returns, no `else`, table-driven tests, etc). This is a more architecturally sensitive fix than usual — follow the pinned design below precisely; every design decision has already been made and verified against the actual code, so you should not need to improvise. If you hit something the spec genuinely doesn't cover, STOP and report back rather than improvising.

## Bug: `merge()` clobbers unset bool/numeric fields, and ignores explicit zero overrides for uint/float

`src/config/merge.go`, function `isZeroValue` (currently lines 166-179):

```go
func isZeroValue(v reflect.Value) bool {
	switch v.Kind() {
	case reflect.Slice, reflect.Map:
		return v.IsNil() || v.Len() == 0
	case reflect.Pointer:
		return v.IsNil()
	case reflect.String:
		return v.String() == ""
	case reflect.Bool, reflect.Int, reflect.Int8, reflect.Int16, reflect.Int32, reflect.Int64:
		return false
	default:
		return v.IsZero()
	}
}
```

Called from `merge(override, base any, skipFields ...string)` (same file, ~line 117-164): `if isZeroValue(overrideField) || !baseField.CanSet() { continue }` — when `isZeroValue` returns false, the field is unconditionally copied from `override` onto `base`.

**Two related bugs, same root cause (field-presence information is discarded before merge runs):**
1. Bool/int kinds always return `false` from `isZeroValue` → ANY bool/int field on `override` unconditionally clobbers `base`, even when the override's source file never mentioned that field at all (so its parsed value is just the Go zero value `false`/`0` by omission, not by explicit choice). Concretely: config A extends config B; B sets `final_space: true`; A's file never mentions `final_space` at all. After `Parse`+`merge`, the result has `FinalSpace: false` — A's *absence* of the field silently overwrites B's explicit `true`.
2. Uint/float kinds fall into the `default: v.IsZero()` branch → the OPPOSITE bug: if override explicitly sets a uint/float field to `0`, that explicit `0` is treated as "absent" and silently ignored, leaving base's non-zero value in place.

Confirmed: `read()` in `src/config/load.go` (~line 195-254) unmarshals each config file straight into a typed `Config` via `yaml.Unmarshal`/`json.Decoder.Decode`/`toml.Unmarshal` — by the time `merge()` runs, there is no remaining signal for "was this key present in the source." A prior implementer working on an unrelated task hit this and worked around it in a test by using only string-typed fields, but did not fix it.

**Fields affected today:** Config — `Async`, `ShellIntegration`, `FinalSpace`, `PatchPwshBleed`, `EnableCursorPositioning`, `Version`, `Streaming`. Block — `Newline`, `Force`, `RestartCycle`, `Index`. Segment — `Newline`, `InvertPowerline`, `Force`, `Toggled`, `Interactive`, `Index`, `MinWidth`, `MaxWidth`, `Timeout`.

**Confirmed constraint — `merge()` is called from exactly two places, verified by grep:** `src/config/load.go:144` (`base.merge(cfg)`, inside `Parse`'s extends loop — the only production call site) and `src/config/merge_test.go` (`tc.baseConfig.merge(tc.overrideConfig)`, twice, on hand-built `*Config` struct literals with NO decode step involved). This means: (a) `merge()` never runs on a config that came from the gob cache — caching only happens after extends-resolution completes, so no presence data needs to survive serialization; (b) the existing `merge_test.go` test cases construct `*Config` literals directly in Go and must keep passing UNCHANGED — your fix must not require editing their expectations. This is only possible if configs built directly as struct literals (no decode step) fall back to TODAY'S behavior (unconditional overwrite for bool/int, ignore-explicit-zero for uint/float) exactly as now, while configs that went through `read()`'s decode step get the corrected, presence-aware behavior. Verify this yourself by reading `merge_test.go` in full before starting — if any existing case would actually need its expectation changed under this design, STOP and report back rather than editing the test to match your implementation.

## Decided design (do not relitigate)

**1. Capture presence at decode time in `read()` (`src/config/load.go`).** After the existing per-format unmarshal into `cfg` succeeds, ALSO decode the same raw `data` bytes generically to discover which top-level keys were present, using the EXISTING `normalize`/`sanitize` helpers already in `src/config/data.go` (lines ~98-142) — do not reinvent this:
   - Unmarshal `data` into a `map[string]any` using the SAME per-format decoder already selected in the `switch cfg.Format` block (yaml/json/toml — for JSON, unmarshal the already-comment-stripped `data` into `map[string]any` directly; `encoding/json` handles this natively).
   - Call `normalize(generic)` (from `data.go`) to get a `map[string]json.RawMessage` keyed by the raw source key names.
   - The set of keys in that map IS your top-level field-presence set for `cfg`.
   - For the `"blocks"` key (if present): `json.Unmarshal(root["blocks"], &rawBlocks)` into `[]map[string]json.RawMessage` (this works because `normalize`+`sanitize` already converted everything to plain JSON-compatible structure). This gives you one presence-set per block, positionally aligned with `cfg.Blocks` (same source, same order — but be defensive: guard with a length check against `len(cfg.Blocks)` before indexing, and skip assigning presence if the lengths don't match rather than panicking).
   - For each block's `"segments"` key (if present): same pattern, one presence-set per segment, positionally aligned with that block's `.Segments`.
   - This decode-and-discard-values approach only cares about KEY PRESENCE, not re-parsing values — don't overthink type fidelity here.

**2. Store presence sets as unexported fields.** Add `presentFields map[string]bool` (unexported, so no json/yaml/toml/gob tag needed — gob only serializes exported fields, and that's fine per the constraint above) to `Config` (`src/config/config.go`), `Block` (`src/config/block.go`), and `Segment` (`src/config/segment.go`). Populate them in `read()` as described above. A nil map is the correct/expected state for struct literals built without going through `read()` (e.g. in tests, or `Default()`) — do not initialize it eagerly elsewhere.

**3. Consult presence in `merge()`, scoped ONLY to the previously-buggy scalar kinds.** In the `merge(override, base any, skipFields ...string)` loop, for fields of kind `Bool`, `Int`/`Int8`/`Int16`/`Int32`/`Int64`, `Uint`/`Uint8`/`Uint16`/`Uint32`/`Uint64`, `Float32`/`Float64`: instead of the current `isZeroValue(overrideField)` check for these kinds specifically, look up whether the field's JSON key was present in `override`'s presence set. Design:
   - Define a small unexported interface, e.g. `type presenceAware interface { fieldPresent(name string) bool }`.
   - Implement `fieldPresent` on `*Config`, `*Block`, `*Segment`: `func (cfg *Config) fieldPresent(name string) bool { if cfg.presentFields == nil { return true }; return cfg.presentFields[name] }` (nil map → always "present", i.e. fall back to today's unconditional-overwrite semantics — this is what keeps `merge_test.go` passing unchanged). Same pattern for `Block`/`Segment`.
   - In `merge()`'s loop, derive the JSON key name for the current field from its struct tag: `field.Tag.Get("json")`, split on `,` and take the first segment (handles `,omitempty`); if empty or `-`, you'll need a sane fallback — check what actually happens for the fields in scope (they should all have proper json tags; if you find one that doesn't, report it, don't guess).
   - For the scalar kinds listed above: `skip := true; if pa, ok := override.(presenceAware); ok { skip = !pa.fieldPresent(jsonKey) } else { skip = isZeroValue(overrideField) }` — i.e., when `override` implements the interface (it always will, since it's always `*Config`/`*Block`/`*Segment` in practice, but keep the type-assertion guard for safety), presence data (even if the map itself is nil, per the nil-safe `fieldPresent` above) decides; otherwise fall back to legacy `isZeroValue`.
   - For all OTHER kinds (string, slice, map, pointer, struct, etc.), leave `isZeroValue`'s existing behavior completely alone — this bug and this fix are scoped to scalar numeric/bool kinds only, do not touch string/slice/map semantics.

**4. `Block.merge` and `Segment.merge` sub-calls.** `Config.merge` calls the generic `merge()` function on override/base pairs for blocks and segments too (see `Config.merge` in `merge.go`, ~lines 62-108: `merge(overrideBlock, cfg.Blocks[i], "Segments")` and `merge(overrideSegment, baseSegment)`). Since `overrideBlock`/`overrideSegment` are `*Block`/`*Segment` and will carry their own `presentFields` (populated per step 1), your `presenceAware` interface implementations on those types make this work automatically through the same generic `merge()` function — no special-casing needed there, just confirm it actually does by testing (step 6 below).

## Files to touch

1. `src/config/data.go` — none, or minimal (reuse `normalize`/`sanitize` as-is; check their exact current signatures before assuming).
2. `src/config/load.go` — extend `read()` with the generic re-decode + presence-population logic described in step 1.
3. `src/config/config.go`, `src/config/block.go`, `src/config/segment.go` — add the unexported `presentFields map[string]bool` field and the `fieldPresent` method to each of `Config`, `Block`, `Segment`.
4. `src/config/merge.go` — implement the `presenceAware` interface type and the scoped presence check in `merge()`'s loop, as described.
5. `src/config/load_test.go` — there's already a `TestParseExtendsChainThreeLevels`-style test using `t.TempDir()` fixture files from a recent commit; follow that exact pattern. Add tests proving, through the REAL `Parse()` pipeline (not hand-built structs — the whole point is this only works via the decode path):
   - Base sets `final_space: true`; override (extending) file omits `final_space` entirely → merged result keeps `true` (the actual reported bug, now fixed).
   - Base sets `final_space: true`; override file explicitly sets `final_space: false` → merged result becomes `false` (explicit override still works, unchanged).
   - Base sets some int field (pick one that's easy to test, e.g. `version` — check it's actually a good candidate, or use a `Block`/`Segment`-level int like a segment's `min_width`) to a non-zero value; override omits it → base's value survives.
   - Base sets a float or uint field (if a convenient one exists at Config/Block/Segment level — check `Segment`'s `MinWidth`/`MaxWidth`/`Timeout` types) to non-zero; override explicitly sets it to `0` → merged result becomes `0` (the second, previously-unfixed bug).
6. `src/config/merge_test.go` — do NOT change existing expectations (per the constraint above). You MAY add new test cases to this file if useful, but only ones that construct configs via a decode step (or explicitly document why a hand-built-literal case is still expected to preserve legacy behavior) — don't retrofit old cases.

## Non-goals — do not touch

- Do not extend presence-tracking to string/slice/map/struct-kind fields — those already work correctly via `isZeroValue`'s existing branches and were not reported broken.
- Do not change `mergeSlices`/`mergeMaps` behavior.
- Do not touch the deprecated `properties`→`options` Segment/TOML migration path — if your presence-tracking logic seems to interact with it, STOP and report back rather than special-casing it yourself.
- Do not add presence tracking anywhere caching/gob-serialization touches — per the confirmed constraint, `merge()` only ever runs pre-cache, so this isn't needed; if you find evidence otherwise, STOP and report back.

## Verification (run from `src/`, must all pass with zero errors before you report done)

```bash
cd src
go build ./...
go test ./config/... -v
modernize --fix "./..."
fieldalignment --fix "./..."
go mod tidy
gofmt -w .
golangci-lint run ./config/...
```
Run `git diff` after the modernize/fieldalignment/gofmt steps and confirm nothing unexpected got rewritten outside the files you touched. Specifically confirm `merge_test.go` needed ZERO edits to its existing test cases (only additions, if any) — if it did need edits, that means the design assumption above was wrong somewhere; report exactly what and why rather than silently patching it to pass.

## Do NOT commit

Leave all changes as uncommitted modifications in the worktree. Do not run `git add` or `git commit` — the orchestrator (a separate process) will review and commit.

## Report back

List every file changed with a one-line description, the exact verification commands you ran and their pass/fail result (paste failures verbatim), confirm whether `merge_test.go`'s existing cases needed edits (should be zero), and explicitly flag anything you had to make a judgment call on or anything the spec didn't fully cover.

content.text: Both fixes are being implemented in parallel, isolated worktrees — the `path.go` fix is small and well-contained; the `merge.go` fix is the riskier one, so I gave it a fully pinned design (I verified the presence-tracking approach against the actual code myself, including the constraint that `merge()` has exactly two call sites, which is what lets the fix stay backward-compatible with the existing test suite).

<result>All verification steps pass. Here is the summary.

## Files changed

1. **`src/regex/regex.go`** — added `FindStringMatchIndex(pattern, text string, index int) (start, end int, ok bool)`, mirroring `FindStringMatch`'s cache lookup and guard style but using `FindStringSubmatchIndex` and checking for `-1` sentinel offsets (non-participating capture group), returning byte-offset pairs instead of the substring text.

2. **`src/regex/regex_test.go`** — added `TestFindStringMatchIndex` with cases for full match, capture group, no match, invalid pattern, out-of-bounds index, non-participating group, and the specific duplicate-text repro (`.*/(src)/deep` against `/home/src/project/src/deep`) confirming the returned span points at the second `src` occurrence, not the first.

3. **`src/segments/path.go`** — in `handleRegex`'s legacy branch (the only branch present in this worktree — see note below), replaced `regex.FindStringMatch` + `strings.Replace` with `regex.FindStringMatchIndex` + a direct slice splice (`input[:start] + pt.mappedLocations[key] + input[end:]`). `path.Clean(input)` and the return shape are unchanged.

4. **`src/segments/path_unix_test.go`** — added the confirmed repro case to `testFullPathCustomMappedLocationsCases`: `Pwd: "/home/src/project/src/deep"`, `MappedLocations: {"re:.*/(src)/deep": "#"}`, `Expected: "/home/src/project/#/deep"`.

5. **`src/segments/path_windows_test.go`** — added a backslash-input sibling case to the same slice, following the file's existing style (`GOOS: runtime.WINDOWS`, `PathSeparator: `\``): `Pwd: `\home\src\project\src\deep``, same regex key, `Expected: `\home\src\project\#\deep``.

## Judgment call — flagging explicitly

The task brief assumes a `mapped_locations_regex_expand=true` sibling branch already exists in `handleRegex` (added by a prior commit) that must be left untouched. That commit (`982337b8 feat(segments): add mapped_locations_regex_expand to path segment`) exists in the repo's overall history but is **not an ancestor of this worktree's HEAD** (`ba1b9eb1`) — `git merge-base --is-ancestor` confirms it. In this checkout, `handleRegex` has only the single legacy branch shown in the bug report; there is no expand branch to avoid touching, and no parallel test structure to mirror in the two test files (I followed the general table style instead). I did not attempt to merge/cherry-pick that unrelated commit in — doing so was out of scope and risked pulling in unrelated changes. If the orchestrator expected this worktree to already include that feature, that's a worktree/base-commit mismatch worth checking before merging this fix.
